headline: Washington forced Anthropic to pull its newest models worldwide while a critical Oracle PeopleSoft zero-day bled gigabytes from hundreds of organizations.

emerging_trends.subject: AI Crossfire
emerging_trends.text: Governments and platforms moved against AI this day, as Washington forced Anthropic's newest models offline and Google sued a network that weaponized Gemini for phishing.

emerging_trends.subject: Supply-Chain Footholds
emerging_trends.text: Poisoned Arch Linux packages and dark-web sales of GitHub access and API keys show attackers increasingly entering through the software supply chain.

emerging_trends.subject: Long Dwell
emerging_trends.text: Velvet Ant's decade inside Linux login software and a 10-year-old phpBB bypass both reward attackers who hide in foundational, rarely inspected code.

emerging_trends.subject: Breach Penalties
emerging_trends.text: Coupang's 409 million dollar fine and the 47 million dollar 23andMe settlement show data breaches now carrying mounting financial consequences.

topics.title: Anthropic Pulls Fable 5 and Mythos 5
topics.last_24h: Anthropic disabled Claude Fable 5 and Mythos 5 worldwide after the US government ordered it to block all foreign nationals from both models, citing national security.
topics.summary: The Trump administration's directive arrived at 5:21 p.m. ET and bars every foreign national from the two models, whether inside or outside the US, so Anthropic suspended access globally for all users. The company complies but disputes the basis, calling the cited jailbreak narrow and the same capability widely available elsewhere. Dark Reading frames Mythos 5 as an upgrade over Mythos Preview and Fable 5 as Mythos made safe for general use.

sources.title: US Gov asks Anthropic to ban 'foreign national' access to Fable, Mythos
U.S. Orders Anthropic to Suspend Fable 5 and Mythos 5 Access for Foreign Nationals
Anthropic Says It Has Taken Its Latest AI Models Offline to Comply With New Export Controls
Claude Fable 5 Doesn't Change the Mythos Security Story

topics.title: Oracle PeopleSoft Zero-Day
topics.last_24h: ShinyHunters exploited a critical zero-day in Oracle's PeopleSoft ERP to steal gigabytes from hundreds of organizations, hitting American universities hardest.
topics.summary: The flaw sits in Oracle-owned PeopleSoft, software that runs human resources and finance for large institutions, and Ars Technica rates it about as critical as vulnerabilities come. ShinyHunters capitalized fast, exfiltrating large troves of data from higher education in particular. Organizations running PeopleSoft should patch and hunt for signs of data theft immediately.

sources.title: PeopleSoft 0-day affecting hundreds of organizations steals gigabytes of data
ShinyHunters Uses Oracle Zero-Day to Rampage Higher Ed

topics.title: Decade-Old phpBB Auth Bypass
topics.last_24h: phpBB patched a 10-year-old authentication bypass that let an attacker log in as any user, administrators included.
topics.summary: phpBB is widely deployed open-source forum software, so the flaw exposed countless community sites for years. The bypass grants full account takeover, including administrative control. Operators should apply the fix at once.

sources.title: phpBB forum fixes auth bypass bug lurking for a decade

topics.title: Arch Linux AUR Hijack
topics.last_24h: Attackers seized more than 400 packages in the Arch User Repository and rewrote their build scripts to drop a Rust infostealer and, with root, an eBPF rootkit.
topics.summary: The AUR is Arch Linux's community-maintained package collection, so any developer who builds a poisoned package runs the malware on their own machine. The Rust binary harvests developer secrets, credentials, and access tokens; when it lands with root it loads an eBPF rootkit to hide itself. Arch users should audit recently built AUR packages and rotate any exposed secrets.

sources.title: Over 400 Arch Linux AUR Packages Hijacked to Deploy Infostealer and eBPF Rootkit
Over 400 Arch Linux packages compromised to push rootkit, infostealer

topics.title: Google Sues Gemini-Abusing Smishing Network
topics.last_24h: Google filed suit against a Chinese cybercrime network it accuses of weaponizing its Gemini AI agent to mass-produce phishing texts aimed at Americans.
topics.summary: Google ties the network to a phishing-as-a-service kit called Outsider, which it says used Gemini to help craft and send smishing messages. The legal action marks Google's attempt to disrupt the operation through the courts. The case underscores how criminals now fold consumer AI agents into commodity phishing toolkits.

sources.title: Google Sues Chinese Smishing Network Accused of Using Gemini AI in Phishing

topics.title: Velvet Ant Linux Login Backdoor
topics.last_24h: Sygnia reported that the China-nexus group Velvet Ant spent close to a decade inside a target's Linux login system, backdooring PAM and OpenSSH to survive ordinary cleanup.
topics.summary: Velvet Ant planted its access in the components that decide who may sign in, a layer defenders rarely inspect. The placement let the group persist for years where routine remediation could not reach. Defenders should verify the integrity of PAM modules and OpenSSH binaries on critical Linux hosts.

sources.title: China-Linked Hackers Backdoored Linux Login Software to Hide for Nearly a Decade

topics.title: Coupang Record Data-Breach Fine
topics.last_24h: South Korea fined e-commerce giant Coupang 409 million dollars over a data breach, the largest personal-data penalty its commission has ever issued.
topics.summary: The fine surpasses the 88.8 million dollar penalty levied against SK Telecom earlier this year, signaling tougher South Korean enforcement. Coupang joins a growing list of companies paying steeply for breach exposure. SecurityWeek flagged the penalty among the week's other notable items, alongside Google security layoffs and an AudiA6 takedown.

sources.title: South Korea hits Coupang with record $409 million fine over data breach
In Other News: Google Security Layoffs, AudiA6 Takedown, $400 Million Coupang Fine

topics.title: Section 702 Surveillance Lapse
topics.last_24h: A legislative deadlock left Section 702 of the Foreign Intelligence Surveillance Act poised to lapse, the program's first expiration since its 2008 passage.
topics.summary: Section 702 authorizes warrantless collection of foreign intelligence and underpins much US signals work. Congress failed to reach agreement before the deadline, so the authority faces its first gap in 18 years. The lapse raises questions over ongoing intelligence operations and the legal footing for collection already underway.

sources.title: Major US surveillance program poised to lapse after legislative deadlock

business_politics.text: Pakistan says the United States and Iran will sign a deal within 24 hours that reopens the Strait of Hormuz, ending a war that began February 28, 2026 and choked Persian Gulf oil and gas shipments. Oil futures settled at multimonth lows and Treasury yields fell on the week as the prospect of peace firmed.

sources.title: Pakistan says US-Iran deal to be signed within 24 hours
Deal With Iran Is Close, but Accord Will Kickstart Toughest Phase of Talks
Oil Futures Fall on Expectations of U.S.-Iran Agreement

business_politics.text: SpaceX raised $75 billion in the largest initial public offering on record, and its shares jumped nearly a fifth on debut to value the rocket and AI company at $2.1 trillion, making Elon Musk the world's first trillionaire.

sources.title: SpaceX's surge on debut makes Musk world's first trillionaire
SpaceX, Now Worth $2.1 Trillion, Pulls Off Goldilocks Debut

business_politics.text: US headline annual inflation reached 4.2%, the highest since April 2023, sharpening the question of when the Federal Reserve can cut rates.

sources.title: Chart of the Week: US headline inflation matters to consumers

business.text: Ford is recalling more than 250,000 Focus models whose engines can stall while driving, after earlier recall repairs failed to fix the defect.

sources.title: Ford recalls more than 250,000 Focus models because engines can stall unexpectedly

business.text: Anthropic pulled its newest AI models, Fable 5 and Mythos 5, offline to comply with a Trump administration directive barring foreign nationals from using them on national-security grounds.

sources.title: Anthropic says it has taken its latest AI models offline to comply with new export controls

around_town.text: Drivers parked along Ward Street in Oakland returned this week to find their cars towed; residents say they got no proper notice, while city officials say the signage had been approved.

sources.title: Oakland residents say cars were towed without proper notice, officials say signage was approved

sources.title: Where the candidates for Pennsylvania governor stand on abortion

around_town.text: Section 702, the federal foreign-surveillance program, expired overnight after Congress failed to pass an extension, raising questions over national security and how companies will handle government data requests.

sources.title: Section 702: Congress just let a surveillance tool expire, but what is it and what's next?

sources.title: Peters Township spray park scheduled to reopen after malfunction caused closure

sources.title: Pogopalooza bounces back to Pittsburgh this weekend

reading.author: Ed Zitron
reading.title: Premium: The Silicon Valley Bubble (Part 1)
reading.summary: Zitron argues the AI era is ending as OpenAI and Anthropic file to go public, racing for exit liquidity even as both burn billions a year with no path to profitability.

reading.author: Stratechery
reading.title: Fable 5, Anthropic Alignment, AI Tiers
reading.summary: Thompson assesses Anthropic's Fable 5, the public version of Mythos, judging it highly capable while warning that its alignment approach and tiered release set troubling precedents.

reading.author: Cal Newport
reading.title: Why Isn't AI Taking Our Jobs?
reading.summary: Newport asks why AI has not eliminated jobs the way industrial machines eliminated manual labor, challenging the comparison AI executives draw to past automation.
